title: Trees
description: Implement TreeNode class, DFS traversals (inorder/preorder/postorder), BFS with Queue, and tree depth calculations.

sections.title: TreeNode Class Definition
sections.explanation: Binary tree problems start with a TreeNode class: `int val`, `TreeNode left`, `TreeNode right`. In interviews, this is usually given but you should know how to write it. Build trees by linking nodes: `root.left = new TreeNode(val)`. Null children represent absent nodes. This is the foundation for all tree problems.

exercise.prompt: Define a TreeNode inner class with int val, TreeNode left, TreeNode right. Build the tree: root=10, left=5, right=15, left.left=3, left.right=7. Print root value, left child value, right child value, and left.right value.

validation.message: Define TreeNode with val, left, and right fields.

exercise.hint: static class TreeNode { int val; TreeNode left, right; TreeNode(int val) { this.val = val; } }

sections.title: DFS Traversals: Inorder, Preorder, Postorder
sections.explanation: Three DFS traversals differ in when you visit the node: Inorder (left, root, right) gives sorted order for BST. Preorder (root, left, right) is used for serialization. Postorder (left, right, root) is used for deletion and evaluation. All three are naturally recursive. Base case: node == null, return.

exercise.prompt: Build a tree: root=1, left=2, right=3, left.left=4, left.right=5. Implement inorder, preorder, and postorder traversals. Print each traversal result as a list.

validation.message: Check for null base case and add node.val to result at the correct position.

exercise.hint: Inorder: recurse left, add val, recurse right. Preorder: add val, recurse left, right. Postorder: recurse left, right, add val.

sections.title: BFS Level-Order Traversal with Queue
sections.explanation: BFS uses a Queue to traverse level by level. For each level: record `queue.size()`, then process that many nodes, adding their children. This gives you access to each level as a separate group—needed for level-order output, right-side view, zigzag traversal. Import LinkedList and Queue.

exercise.prompt: Build a tree: root=1, left=2, right=3, left.left=4, left.right=5, right.left=6, right.right=7. Implement BFS level-order traversal and print each level as a list. Print the result of all levels.

validation.message: Use a Queue with offer/poll and size() for level-by-level BFS.

exercise.hint: Use queue.size() to process one level at a time. For each node, add its non-null children to the queue.

sections.title: Tree Depth and Height
sections.explanation: Max depth (height) of a tree is the longest root-to-leaf path. Recursive formula: `maxDepth(node) = 1 + max(maxDepth(left), maxDepth(right))`. Base case: null node returns 0. Min depth is the shortest root-to-leaf path (be careful: must reach an actual leaf). These are fundamental building blocks for balanced tree checks and diameter calculations.

exercise.prompt: Build a tree: root=1, left=2, right=3, left.left=4, left.right=5, left.left.left=8. Write maxDepth and print it. Also write a method countNodes that counts total nodes in the tree and print that.

validation.message: Max depth is 4 (1->2->4->8). Total nodes is 6.
Use Math.max for depth and null check as base case.

exercise.hint: maxDepth: null returns 0, else 1 + max(left, right). countNodes: null returns 0, else 1 + count(left) + count(right).
